content.title: Synaesthesia
content.summary: {'A': '这段话主要讲述了一个页面上有一个方框，方框中有一些重复的数字5，但隐藏其中的一个大写字母S，只有具有字母和数字颜色不同感知能力的人才能很快地找到这个字母S。', 'B': '这段话主要讲了有关感官交叉感知的现象，即合并不同感官的能力，这种现象是一种神经学上的有趣现象，它可能导致词语具有特定的味道，或者某些气味会产生特定的颜色，甚至每个字母和数字都有自己的个性，而且这些感官关联通常是无意识且无法抑制的，并且通常随时间保持相对稳定。', 'C': '这段话主要讲述了早在19世纪初，德国医生乔治·萨克斯首次记录了自己对这种病症的经历，但直到20世纪90年代中期，西蒙·巴伦-科恩教授和他的同事使用fMRls技术对六名感觉通感者进行实证研究，发现与视觉相关的大脑部位在听觉刺激时也会活跃，即使被盲目蒙住眼睛。', 'D': '这段话主要讲了多感觉症是一种有趣的条件，不是疾病，而且被认为是一种祝福，与智力和创造力有关，一些名人也声称自己有这种症状。', 'E': '这段话主要讲了关于辞素-颜色共感的另一个有趣方面，即它可能有助于未来的后代。', 'F': '教授人们创造字母-颜色联想可能有助于提高认知功能和记忆，甚至可能预防老年人认知衰退。', 'G': '这段话主要讲述了对于共感觉现象的理解仍然有很长的路要走，但一旦我们理解了它的原因，可能很快就能找到一种方法来教会非共感觉者如何模仿其症状，以获得目前全球4.4%人口所享受的同样好处。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Imagine a page with a square box in the middle. The box is lined with rows of the number 5, repeated over and over. All of the 5s are identical in size, font and colour, and equally distributed across the box. There is, however, a trick: among those 5s, hiding in plain sight is a single, capital letter S. Almost the same in shape, it is impossible to spot without straining your eyes for a good few minutes. Unless that is, you are a grapheme – colour synaesthete – a person who sees each letter and number in dierent colours. With all the 5s painted in one colour and the rogue S painted in another, a grapheme – colour synaesthete will usually only need a split second to identify the latter.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Synaesthesia, loosely translated as "senses coming together" from the Greek words syn("with") and aesthesis ("sensation"), is an interesting neurological phenomenon that causes dierent senses to be combined. This might mean that words have a particular taste (for example, the word "door" might taste like bacon), or that certain smells produce a particular colour. It might also mean that each letter and number has its own personality-the letter A might be perky, the letter B might be shy and self-conscious, etc. Some synaesthetes might even experience other people's sensations, for example feeling pain in their chest when they witness a film character gets shot. The possibilities are endless: even though synaesthesia is believed to aeeect less than 5% of the general population, at least 60 dieeerent combinations of senses have been reported so far. What all these sensory associations have in common is that they are all involuntary and impossible to repress and that they usually remain quite stable over time.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Synaesthesia was first documented in the early 19th century by German physician Georg Sachs, who dedicated two pages of his dissertation on his own experience with the condition. It wasn't, however, until the mid-1990s that empirical research proved its existence when Professor Simon Baron-Cohen and his colleagues used fMRls on six synaesthetes and discovered that the parts of the brain associated with vision were active during auditory stimulation, even though the subjects were blindfolded.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: What makes synaesthesia a particularly interesting condition is that it isn't an illness at all. If anything, synaesthetes often report feeling sorry for the rest of the population, as they don't have the opportunity to experience the world in a multisensory fashion like they do. Very few drawbacks have been described, usually minimal: for instance, some words might have an unpleasant taste (imagine the word "hello" tasting like spoilt milk), while some synaesthetes find it distressing when they encounter people with names which don't reflect their personality(imagine meeting a very interesting person named "Lee", when the letter E has a dull or hideous colour for you-or vice versa). Overall, however, synaesthesia is widely considered more of a blessing than a curse and it is often linked to intelligence and creativity, with celebrities such as Lady Gaga and Pharrell Williams claiming to have it.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: Another fascinating side of synaesthesia is the way it could potentially benefit future generations. In a 2013 study, Dr Witthof and Dr Winawer discovered that grapheme-colour synaesthetes who had never met each other before experienced strikingly similar pairings between graphemes and colours-pairings which were later traced back to a popular set of Fischer-Price magnets that ten out of eleven participants distinctly remembered possessing as children. This was particularly peculiar as synaesthesia is predominantly considered to be a hereditary condition, and the findings suggested that a synaesthete's environment might play a determining role in establishing synaesthetic associations. If that was true, researchers asked, then might it not be possible that synaesthesia can actually be taught?

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: As it turns out, the benefits of teaching synaesthesia would be tremendous. According to research conducted by Dr Clare Jonas at the University of East London, teaching people to create grapheme-colour associations the same way as a synaesthete may have the possibility to improve cognitive function and memory. As she put it, 'one possibility is guarding against cognitive decline in older people-using synaesthesia in the creation of mnemonics to remember things such as shopping lists.' To that end, researchers in the Netherlands have already begun developing a web browser plug-in that will change the colours of certain letters. Rothen and his colleagues corroborate the theory: in a paper published in 2011, they suggest that synaesthesia might be more than a hereditary condition, as the non-synaesthetic subjects of their study were able to mimic synaesthetic associations long after leaving the lab.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: There is obviously still a long way to go before we can fully understand synaesthesia and what causes it. Once we do, however, it might not be too long before we find out how to teach non-synaesthetes how to imitate its symptoms in a way that induces the same benefits 4.4% of the world's population currently enjoy.

questions.title: Before Professor Simon Baron-Cohen’s research, synaesthesia was thought to be a myth.
questions.analysis:
题干中提到了"Before Professor Simon Baron-Cohen’s research, synaesthesia was thought to be a myth."，即在Simon Baron-Cohen教授的研究之前，人们认为色觉通感是一个神话。我们需要在文章中找到相关信息来判断这个说法是否正确。

在文章中，我们可以找到关于synaesthesia的信息，但是并没有提到在Simon Baron-Cohen教授的研究之前人们是否认为它是一个神话。文章中提到了synaesthesia最早是在19世纪初由德国医生Georg Sachs记录的，然后在1990年代中期，Simon Baron-Cohen教授和他的同事使用fMRls技术对六名色觉通感者进行了研究，并发现与视觉相关的大脑部位在听觉刺激时也会活跃，即使被试者被蒙上了眼睛。

由于文章中没有提到在Simon Baron-Cohen教授的研究之前人们是否认为synaesthesia是一个神话，所以我们无法确定这个说法的真实性。因此，答案是"NOT GIVEN"，即无法确定。

central_sentences.sentence: Synaesthesia was first documented in the early 19th century by German physician Georg Sachs, who dedicated two pages of his dissertation on his own experience with the condition. It wasn’t, however, until the mid-1990s that empirical research proved its existence when Professor Simon Baron-Cohen and his colleagues used fMRls on six synaesthetes and discovered that the parts of the brain associated with vision were active during auditory stimulation, even though the subjects were blindfolded
central_sentences.translation: 辞海感觉通感（Synaesthesia）最早在19世纪初由德国医生乔治·萨克斯（Georg Sachs）首次记录下来，他在自己的论文中专门用了两页来描述自己对这种状况的体验。然而，直到20世纪90年代中期，当西蒙·巴伦-科恩教授和他的同事们对六名感觉通感者进行功能磁共振成像（fMRI）研究时，才通过实证研究证明了其存在。研究发现，即使被盲目蒙住眼睛，与视觉相关的大脑部位在听觉刺激时也会活跃。

Synaesthesia was first documented in the early 19th century by German physician Georg Sachs, who dedicated two pages of his dissertation on his own experience with the condition. It wasn’t, however, until the mid-1990s that empirical research proved its existence when Professor Simon Baron-Cohen and his colleagues used fMRls on six synaesthetes and discovered that the parts of the brain associated with vision were active during auditory stimulation, even though the subjects were blindfolded.

content.title: Crisis! freshwater
content.summary: {'A': '全球各地的决策者对水资源的管理拥有巨大的权力，明智地利用这种权力将变得越来越重要，因为世界对淡水的需求目前已经超过了许多地方的供应，而且这种情况没有减轻的迹象。', 'B': '这段话主要讲述了全球淡水资源短缺的问题，预计到2025年，超过一半的国家将面临水资源紧缺或压力，到本世纪中叶，全球约三分之四的人口可能面临淡水短缺。', 'C': '科学家预计，水资源短缺将变得更加普遍，主要原因是全球人口增长和许多人变得富裕（从而扩大需求），以及全球气候变化加剧了干旱并减少了许多地区的供水，此外，许多水源受到错误废物处理、工业污染物排放、化肥流失和海水入侵等威胁。', 'D': '缺乏水资源会导致饥饿、疾病、政治不稳定和武装冲突，因此需要采取行动来保证水资源安全。', 'E': '这段话主要讲述了全球水问题需要了解每个人所需淡水量以及不同地区供应障碍和需求增加的因素，并指出每个人至少需要1000立方米的水，相当于两个五分之一的奥运游泳池的容量。', 'F': '该段落主要讲述了美洲和北欧亚地区拥有丰富的水资源，但一些地区面临着不同程度的“实际”水资源匮乏问题，需求超过当地供应；而中非、印度次大陆和东南亚等地区则面临“经济”水资源匮乏问题，尽管供应充足但无法获得足够的水资源。', 'G': '这段话主要讲述了地表降水中超过一半的水分因蒸发或植物蒸腾而无法被捕获或储存，这部分水被称为蓝水资源，包括河流、湖泊、湿地和含水层，人们可以直接利用。农田灌溉是淡水资源最大的单一人类利用方式，但其强烈的地方需求常常耗尽周围的可用水源。', 'H': '全球每年有110,000立方千米的降水，但大部分无法被利用，只有1.5%直接被人类使用。', 'I': '近年来，大规模的野火导致水资源流失，经济行为者合理地分配了水资源，但却没有考虑到自然环境的需求，导致干旱使得水资源供应不足，自然环境遭受了巨大的损害。澳大利亚的Murray-Darling盆地委员会成员现在正在拼命努力摆脱他们对总水资源的错误分配所带来的灾难性后果。在一个极度干旱的地区，像约旦河流域这样的国际河流流域的水资源分配更加复杂，该流域与黎巴嫩、叙利亚、以色列、巴勒斯坦地区和约旦接壤，所有这些地区都对有限的共享水资源有要求。淡水的争夺已经导致该地区的内部冲突和军事争端，只有持续的谈判和妥协才能保持这种紧张局势的控制。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: . As in New Delhi and Phoenix, policymakers worldwide wield great power over how water resources and managed. Wise use of such power will become increasingly important as the years go by because the world's demand for freshwater is currently overtaking its ready supply in many places, and this situation shows no sign of abating.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: . That the problem is well-known makes it no less disturbing: today one out of six people, more than a billion, suffer inadequate access to safe freshwater. By 2025, according to data released by the United Nations, the freshwater resources of more than half the countries across the globe will undergo either stress- for example, when people increasingly demand more water than is available or safe for use-or outright shortages. By mid-century, as much as three-quarters of the earth's population could face scarcities of freshwater.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: . Scientists expect water scarcity to become more common in large part because the world's population is rising and many people are getting richer (thus expanding demand) and because global climate change is exacerbating aridity and reducing supply in many regions. What is more, many water sources are threatened by faulty waste disposal, releases of industrial pollutants, fertilizer runoff, and coastal influxes of saltwater into aquifers as groundwater is depleted.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: . Because lack of access to water can lead to starvation, disease, political instability, and even armed conflict, failure to take action can have broad and grave consequences. Fortunately, to a great extent, the technologies and policy tools required to conserve existing freshwater and to secure more of it are known among which several seem particularly effective. What is needed now is action. Governments and authorities at every level have to formulate and execute plans for implementing the political, economic, and technological measures that can ensure water security now and in the coming decades.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: . The world's water problems require, as a start, an understanding of how much freshwater each person requires, along with knowledge of the factors that impede supply and increase demand in different parts of the world. Main Falkenmark of the Stockholm International Water Institute and other experts estimate that, on average, each person on the earth needs a minimum of 1000 cubic meters (m3) of water. The minimum water each person requires for drinking, hygiene, and growing food. The volume is equivalent to two-fifths of an Olympic-size swimming pool.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: . Much of the Americas and northern Eurasia enjoy abundant water supplies. But several regions are beset by greater or lesser degrees of "physical" scarcity-whereby demand exceeds local availability. Other areas, among them Central Africa, parts of the Indian subcontinent, and Southeast Asia contend with "economic" water scarcity limit access even though sufficient supplies are available.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: More than half of the precipitation that falls on land is never available for capture or storage because it evaporates from the ground or transpires from plants; this fraction is called blue-water sources-rivers, lakes, wetlands, and aquifers-that people can tap directly. Farm irrigation from these free-flowing bodies is the biggest single human use of freshwater resources, but the intense local demand they create often drains the surroundings of ready supplies.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: . Lots of water, but not always where it is needed one hundred and ten thousand cubic kilometers of precipitation, nearly 10 times the volume of Lake Superior, falls from the sky onto the earth's land surface every year. This huge quantity would easily fulfill the requirements of everyone on the planet if the water arrived where and when people needed it. But much of it cannot be captured (top), and the rest is disturbed unevenly (bottom). Green water (61.1% of total precipitation): absorbed by soil and plants, then released back into the air: unavailable for withdrawal. Blue water (38.8% of total precipitation): collected in rivers, lakes, wetlands, and groundwater: available for withdrawal before it evaporates or reaches the ocean. These figures may not add up to 100% because of rounding. Only 1.5% is directly used by people.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: . Waters run away in tremendous wildfires in recent years. The economic actors had all taken their share reasonably enough: they just did not consider the needs of the natural environment, which suffered greatly when its inadequate supply was reduced to critical levels by drought. The members of the Murray-Darling Basin Commission are now frantically trying to extricate themselves from the disastrous results of their misallocation of the total water resource. Given the difficulties of sensibly apportioning the water supply within a single nation, imagine the complexities of doing so for international river basins such as that of the Jordan River, which borders on Lebanon, Syria, Israel, the Palestinian areas, and Jordan, all of which have claims to the shared, but limited, supply in an extremely parched region. The struggle for freshwater has contributed to civil and military disputes in the area. Only continuing negotiations and compromises have kept this tense situation under control.

content.summary: {'A': '这段话主要讲述了澳大利亚最著名的海滩邦迪海滩的位置和其原住民的文化遗产。', 'B': '悉尼地区的土著居民被称为Eora，他们的语言可能是Darug语言的一个分支，也可能是一种独立的语言群体，而悉尼地区的一些地名则源自悉尼地区土著语言的词汇。', 'C': '这段话主要讲述了澳大利亚邦迪海滩的历史，包括海滩的起源、土地的购买和命名、以及政府干预以确保海滩对公众开放的故事。', 'D': '这段话主要讲述了在20世纪，海滩与健康、休闲和民主联系在一起，成为每个人都能平等享受的游乐场所，而邦迪海滩在20世纪大部分时间都是一个工人阶级的郊区，新西兰移民占据了当地人口的大多数，1884年第一条有轨电车到达海滩后，电车成为邦迪的第一种公共交通工具，这一举动改变了只有富人才能享受海滩的规则，到了20世纪30年代，邦迪不仅吸引了当地游客，还吸引了来自澳大利亚其他地方和海外的人们，当时的广告将邦迪海滩称为“太平洋的游乐场”。', 'E': '这段话主要讲述了人们越来越倾向于在海边放松而不是在城市中不健康地生活的趋势，以及海滩游泳的普及引发的公共安全问题和如何防止人们溺水的措施，其中包括世界上第一个正式记录的冲浪救生俱乐部——邦迪冲浪浴场救生俱乐部的成立，以及1938年邦迪海滩的“黑色星期天”事件，该事件中有35,000人在海滩上，一大群救生员正准备开始冲浪比赛时，三个突发的巨浪袭击了海滩，将数百人冲到了海里，救生员成功营救了300人，这是冲浪游泳历史上最大规模的集体营救行动，进一步确认了救生员在国家想象中的地位。', 'F': '这段话主要讲述了邦迪海滩作为悉尼城市到冲浪乐跑的终点站，以及其作为澳大利亚冲浪嘉年华的重要地点，同时提到了英国皇室成员对澳大利亚的多次访问，以及邦迪海滩每周日开放的市集和圣诞节时人们在海滩度过的情况，但也提到了人们涌向海滩时住房短缺的问题，而曼利则成为了解决这个问题的海滨小镇，但人们仍然更喜欢选择邦迪作为满意的目的地。', 'G': '这段话主要讲述了澳大利亚邦迪海滩的商业区和房屋建筑受到欧洲和英国沿海城镇的深刻影响。', 'H': '这段话主要讲述了2000年夏季奥运会在邦迪海滩举办了沙滩排球比赛，为此搭建了临时的万座体育馆和其他场地，但这一举措引发了社会和环境方面的争议，人们抗议并主张保护邦迪海滩的纯净性和健康生活。', 'I': '这段话主要讲述了人们为了八天的排球比赛而愿意冒着生命风险和危害邦迪海滩环境的可能性，同时还存在土壤被挖掘上来后酸化的环境担忧。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Bondi Beach, Australia's most famous beach, is located in the suburb of Bondi, in the Local Government Area of Waverley, seven kilometers from the centre of Sydney. "Bondi" or"Boondi" is an Aboriginal word meaning water breaking over rocks or the sound of breaking waves. The Australian Museum records that Bondi means a place where a flight of nullas took place. There are Aboriginal Rock carving on the northern end of the beach at Ben Buckler and south of Bondi Beach near McKenzies Beach on the coastal walk.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: The indigenous people of the area at the time of European settlement have generally been welcomed to as the Sydney people or the Eora (Eora means "the people"). One theory describes the Eora as a sub-group of the Darug language group which occupied the Cumberland Plain west to the Blue Mountains. However, another theory suggests that they were a distinct language group of their own. There is no clear evidence for the name or names of the particular band(s) of the Eora that roamed what is now the Waverley area. A number of place names within Waverley, most famously Bondi, have been based on words derived from Aboriginal languages of the Sydney region.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: From the mid-1800s Bondi Beach was a favourite location for family outings and picnics. The beginnings of the suburb go back to 1809, when the early road builder, William Roberts, received from Governor Bligh a grant of 81 hectares of what is now most of the business and residential area of Bondi Beach. In 1851, Edward Smith Hall and Francis O'Brien purchased 200 acres of the Bondi area that embraced almost the whole frontage of Bondi Beach, and it was named the "The Bondi Estate." Between 1855 and 1877 O'Brien purchased Hall's share of the land, renamed the land the "O'Brien Estate," and made the beach and the surrounding land available to the public as a picnic ground and amusement resort. As the beach became increasingly popular, O'Brien threatened to stop public beach access. However, the Municipal Council believed that the Government needed to intervene to make the beach a public reserve.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: During the 1900s beach became associated with health, leisure and democracy – a playground everyone could enjoy equally. Bondi Beach was a working-class suburb throughout most of the twentieth century with migrant people from New Zealand comprising the majority of the local population. The first tramway reached the beach in 1884. Following this, tram became the first public transportation in Bondi. As an alternative, this action changed the rule that only rich people can enjoy the beach. By the 1930s Bondi was drawing not only local visitors but also people from elsewhere in Australia and overseas. Advertising at the time referred to Bondi Beach as the "Playground of the Pacific".

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: There is a growing trend that people prefer having to relax near seaside instead of living unhealthily in cities. The increasing popularity of sea bathing during the late 1800s and early 1900s raised concerns about public safety and how to prevent people from drowning. In response, the world's first formally documented surf lifesaving club, the Bondi Surf Bathers'Life Saving Club, was formed in 1907. This was powerfully reinforced by the dramatic events of "Black Sunday" at Bondi in 1938. Some 35,000 people were on the beach and a large group of lifesavers were about to start a surf race when three freak waves hit the beach, sweeping hundreds of people out to sea. Lifesavers rescued 300 people. The largest mass rescue in the history of surf bathing, it confirmed the place of the lifesaver in the national imagination.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Bondi Beach is the endpoint of the City to Surf Fun Run which is held each year in August. Australian surf carnivals further instilled this image. A Royal Surf Carnival was held at Bondi Beach for Queen Elizabeth II during her first visited in Australia in 1954. Since 1867, there have been over fifty visits by a member of the British Royal Family to Australia. In addition to many activities, the Bondi Beach Markets is open every Sunday. Many wealthy people spend Christmas Day at the beach. However, the shortage of houses occurs when lots of people crushed to the seaside. Manly is the seashore town which solved this problem. However, people still choose Bondi as the satisfied destination rather than Manly.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: Bondi Beach has a commercial area along Campbell Parade and adjacent side streets, featuring many popular cafes, restaurants, and hotels, with views of the contemporary beach. It is depicted as wholly modern and European. In the last decade, Bondi Beaches' unique position has seen a dramatic rise in svelte houses and apartments to take advantage of the views and scent of the sea. The valley running down to the beach is the famous world over for its view of distinctive red-tiled roofs. Those architectures are deeply influenced by British coastal town.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Bondi Beach hosted the beach volleyball competition at the 2000 Summer Olympics. A temporary 10,000-seat stadium, a much smaller stadium, 2 warm-up courts, and 3 training courts were set up to host the tournament. The Bondi Beach Volleyball Stadium was constructed for it and stood for just six weeks. Campaigners oppose both the social and environmental consequences of the development. The stadium will divide the beach in two and seriously restrict public access for swimming, walking, and other forms of outdoor recreation. People protest for their human rights of having a pure seaside and argue for health life in Bondi.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: "They're prepared to risk lives and risk the Bondi beach environment for the sake of eight days of volleyball", said Stephen Uniacke, a construction lawyer involved in the campaign. Other environmental concerns include the possibility that soil dredged up from below the sand will acidify when brought to the surface.

questions.analysis:
题干要求我们找出19世纪末人们去邦迪海滩时使用的公共交通工具。我们可以通过以下步骤快速得到答案"tram"：

2. 在原文中，我们可以找到一句话："The first tramway reached the beach in 1884."（第一条有轨电车在1884年到达海滩。）

3. 这句话告诉我们，19世纪末，人们使用的公共交通工具是有轨电车（tramway）。

因此，答案是"tram"。

2. 接下来，我们需要找到与"first visit"相关的信息。在原文中，我们找到了这样一句话："A Royal Surf Carnival was held at Bondi Beach for Queen Elizabeth II during her first visited in Australia in 1954." 这句话告诉我们，1954年，女王伊丽莎白二世首次访问澳大利亚时，在邦迪海滩举行了一次皇家冲浪嘉年华。

因此，答案是1954。
